Call 2025 - Round 3
Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-8824E0EB Deadline (Brussels Time) 01 Oct 2025 12:00:00
Project Summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
The "Natural Beauties" project aims to empower young people by deepening their understanding of climate change and
equipping them with the tools and skills needed to take concrete action in their local communities. The project is designed to
bridge the gap between awareness and action by combining knowledge-sharing with hands-on environmental engagement.
Through non-formal and experiential learning, participants will not only gain theoretical insights into climate issues but also
develop practical competencies that will enable them to become active changemakers in their societies. The project is
rooted in youth work practices that emphasize peer-to-peer learning, intercultural collaboration, and real-world problem-
solving. One of the core objectives is to help participants recognize their role in combating climate change and to inspire
them to adopt sustainable lifestyles. Many young people today are aware of environmental challenges but often lack clear
guidance on how to translate their concerns into meaningful action. This project provides a structured framework where
participants can explore sustainable practices in an interactive and supportive environment. They will engage in activities
such as guided nature explorations, community clean-up events, and sustainability workshops where they will be introduced
to simple yet impactful behavioral changes, such as reducing waste, minimizing their carbon footprint, and advocating for
green policies in their communities. To achieve these objectives, the project integrates a variety of hands-on activities that
ensure deep learning and active participation. For example, during the "Green Exploration" activity, participants will embark
on an outdoor learning experience, where they will analyze the biodiversity of a natural area, identify environmental threats,
and discuss local conservation strategies. This experiential approach helps them understand ecological interdependencies
firsthand and supports a sense of responsibility for nature conservation. Another key activity is the "Waste Collection &
Recycling Challenge," where participants will visit a public space affected by pollution and collectively work on restoring its
natural state by cleaning up litter. They will then engage in a creative upcycling session, where they will transform discarded
materials into useful or artistic items, demonstrating the potential of recycling in an engaging and hands-on manner.
Workshops on sustainable living will also be a fundamental part of the project. Sessions will cover topics such as zero-waste
lifestyles, energy conservation, and ethical consumption. Through discussions and interactive demonstrations, participants
will learn how small changes—such as reducing single-use plastics, making informed food choices, and opting for eco-
friendly transportation—can collectively have a significant impact on the environment. They will also have opportunities to
share their own experiences and brainstorm solutions tailored to their local contexts. Another key element of the project is
nurturing youth empowerment through collaboration. Many young people feel disconnected from environmental decision-
making processes, but this project seeks to change that by providing them with leadership opportunities. Throughout the
exchange, participants will be encouraged to take ownership of specific activities, facilitating discussions, leading group
exercises, and even co-designing awareness campaigns. For instance, the "Youth for Climate Advocacy" session will
challenge participants to create short, persuasive messages that can be shared on social media to raise awareness about
climate issues. These campaigns will be published on project partners’ social media channels, extending the project’s reach
beyond the immediate participants. Cultural exchange and teamwork are also integral to the project’s success. By bringing
together young people from diverse backgrounds, the initiative creates cross-cultural understanding and cooperation. This
will be achieved through activities like "Policy Proposal," where participants will share insights into how environmental
sustainability is approached in their home countries, exchange best practices, and explore ways to integrate global and local
perspectives into their own sustainability efforts. By doing so, they not only expand their knowledge but also build a sense of
solidarity and shared responsibility for the planet. To ensure the long-term impact of the project, a structured follow-up
strategy will be implemented. Each participant will be encouraged to develop a personal action plan outlining concrete steps
they will take upon returning home. They will also be tasked with organizing at least one local event, such as a community
clean-up, an awareness workshop, or a presentation at a school or youth center, to share their newly acquired knowledge
and experiences. This approach reinforces the principle of knowledge multiplication.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
The program consists of interactive, hands-on activities designed to engage participants in environmental awareness,
sustainability practices, and intercultural learning through non-formal education. It begins with preparatory activities,
including an online pre-assessment survey and a virtual introduction where participants share expectations and discuss
environmental issues in their countries. A digital platform will be created for resource sharing, and group leaders will attend
an orientation session to clarify roles and logistics. Upon arrival, participants will take part in team-building and ice-breaking
exercises to create trust and inclusion. The “Eco-Challenge” will require them to collaborate on solving a simulated
environmental crisis, encouraging problem-solving and teamwork. This phase ensures they feel comfortable in a diverse
international setting. The core activities emphasize experiential learning. The "Green Exploration" session includes guided
hikes in nature reserves and urban green spaces, where participants assess biodiversity and environmental impact. They
will learn about conservation techniques, sustainable land management, and practical ways to protect ecosystems. This

activity deepens their connection with nature and provides real-world context for sustainability discussions. In the “Waste
Collection and Recycling” initiative, participants will collect litter from urban and natural areas, analyzing the waste types to
discuss local environmental policies. To promote circular economy principles, they will create upcycled products from
collected waste, such as reusable bags or art pieces, which will be displayed in a final exhibition. Additionally, they will learn
composting techniques and discuss waste reduction strategies. The program includes interactive workshops on
environmental advocacy, eco-entrepreneurship, and digital tools for sustainability. Participants will explore grassroots
environmental movements, analyze successful campaigns, and develop strategies for their own activism. They will be
introduced to digital mapping tools for tracking pollution and will design social media campaigns to raise awareness about
climate issues. They will have two sessions to prepare together a policy proposal about the discussed sustainability
initiatives. A cultural exchange component will encourage knowledge-sharing about global environmental challenges. Each
national group will present an environmental issue specific to their country, followed by discussions on collaborative
solutions. This activity supports intercultural understanding and encourages participants to apply international perspectives
to local problems. YouthPass Reflection Sessions will be held to help participants recognize their learning progress. Instead
of simple discussions, they will create digital or physical portfolios documenting their acquired skills, key insights, and post-
project action plans. This method ensures long-term impact by encouraging participants to continue applying their
knowledge. The project concludes with a community engagement event, where participants will present sustainability action
plans developed during the program. These plans will be tailored for different stakeholders (schools, businesses, local
authorities) and will be showcased at a "Sustainable Futures Fair," featuring interactive booths, demonstrations, and
workshops led by participants. This ensures that the program’s impact extends beyond its immediate participants. The
project involves 35 young people (18-30) from Hungary, Poland, Portugal, Ukraine, and Germany, with five participants and
one group leader from each country. Two experienced facilitators from Hungarian NGOs will provide mentorship and ensure
inclusive learning experiences. Special attention will be given to involving participants with fewer opportunities, ensuring
diversity and accessibility.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
The expected results and impact of the project are multifaceted, extending from the individual participants to local
communities, partner organizations, and even a broader European level. One of the primary outcomes is increasing young
people's awareness of environmental challenges, but beyond general knowledge, participants will engage in hands-on
activities such as waste collection, upcycling workshops, and ecological excursions. These experiences will not only provide
practical knowledge but also boost a sense of responsibility and agency in taking action for environmental protection. For
instance, by engaging in "Green Exploration" sessions, participants will have the opportunity to experience firsthand the
impact of human activity on natural ecosystems and explore real-world solutions, such as biodiversity conservation and
sustainable waste management. At the individual level, participants will develop key competences in sustainability,
leadership, teamwork, and critical thinking. Through structured reflection sessions, such as “Self-Reflection Time” and
interactive discussions like “Fears, Expectations, Challenges,” young people will analyze their own behaviors and consider
how to implement more sustainable habits in their daily lives. These competencies will not only be recognized through the
Youthpass certificate but also will have practical applications in their personal and professional lives. A participant who
becomes more conscious of waste management, for example, may return home and start a recycling initiative in their
community or influence their peers through social media advocacy. Beyond the direct participants, the project is designed to
create a multiplier effect. Each young person will be encouraged to share their experiences and learnings with their home
communities through small-scale local initiatives. This could involve organizing awareness campaigns, leading workshops in
schools, or collaborating with local environmental organizations. The project’s impact will be amplified through a strong
dissemination plan, ensuring that best practices and lessons learned are shared widely. In addition to posting final outcomes
on social media and project websites, they will be integrated into platforms such as SALTO, making them accessible to
other NGOs, educators, and youth workers who can replicate or adapt them in their own contexts. At the organizational
level, partner NGOs will benefit from strengthened collaboration, improved project management skills, and new
methodologies for youth engagement. The experience gained in this project will serve as a foundation for future international
collaborations, reinforcing their ability to implement high-quality youth mobility projects. The project will also establish a
network of young environmental advocates across multiple countries, facilitating continued knowledge exchange and
collaboration even after the project officially ends. From a regional and European perspective, the project aims to contribute
to a larger movement of youth-led environmental action. By connecting participants from different cultural backgrounds, it
will promote European values of cooperation, inclusion, and active citizenship. The structured use of digital tools in
dissemination, including video documentaries, blogs, and interactive content, will ensure that the project's messages reach
a broad audience. Moreover, engagement with local municipalities, educational institutions, and environmental
organizations will create lasting partnerships that can continue addressing sustainability challenges in a more coordinated
manner. Long-term sustainability is another critical impact area. The project will establish concrete follow-up measures to
ensure that its effects do not fade after the mobility ends. Participants will be encouraged to form local eco-action groups,
organize reunions to track their progress, and contribute to an ongoing online platform where they can exchange ideas and
resources. Additionally, specific impact measurement indicators will be used to assess the behavioral changes among
participants and their communities, including tracking the implementation of sustainable habits and the creation of new
environmental initiatives. In essence, the project is designed not only to educate but also to inspire action, equipping young
people with the skills, motivation, and network to drive real environmental change. Through a combination of practical
engagement, reflective learning, and strategic dissemination, it will support a new generation of young leaders dedicated to

Please briefly present your organisation/the group.
The Street of Youth Association is a youth organization based in Budapest, Hungary, and composed of Generation Z

members, specifically focusing on the challenges, problems, and issues faced by Generation Z youth (18-25 years old
youngsters). Our specialty is that we achieve this primarily by focusing on international projects and non-formal education.
We believe in the power of non-formal education and in the power of Erasmus+. We understand that Gen Z young people
are greatly helped through these project, especially with starting their careers, finding their path, and developing their soft
skills.
Our members are exclusively Generation Z youth who want to contribute to their own community and support other young
people. In the association, both the management and the local programs, as well as the international projects, are carried
out by young people, for young people. We believe this is the most effective way to truly make an impact on the lives of other
young people, engage with them and have a positive influence on our environment.
We can identify with the fact that this generation has completely different needs and is driven by different topics compared
to previous generations. Accordingly, there are three topics that we prioritize and consider the most important in our
organization: the mental health of Gen Z young people; environment protection and sustainability; and the challenges related
to the digital world, particularly social media.
We also believe in youth empowerment. It is important to mention that our association operates on a similar principle to a
startup: if any of our members have a good idea or a passion project, the Street of Youth Association provides the
opportunity and a supportive environment for them to realize it.
What are the activities and experience of the organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your organisation’s
/ group’s regular youth work activities.
Street of Youth Association is a youth-led organiza1on committed to providing high-quality non-formal learning opportunities
for young people. Established in February 2023, the association is composed of university students and young professionals
with significant experience in Erasmus+ youth exchanges, training courses, and youth participation projects. The
organization works to address key challenges faced by young people today while promoting international mobility and
meaningful learning experiences.
One of the main focus areas is mental health and well-being, particularly in relation to work-life balance, career-related
stress, and excessive online presence. To address these concerns, the association is currently developing a podcast series,
which is led by young people and features expert insights. This initiative aims to raise awareness and provide practical
solutions for young people struggling with digital challenges and maintaining a healthy work-life balance.
Environmental protection and sustainability are also central themes in the association’s activities.
In addition to preparing an Erasmus+ Youth Exchange on sustainable habits planned for 2025, several sustainability-
focused workshops and actions have already been implemented. A session in a zero-waste coffee shop introduced
participants to practical ways of reducing daily waste and adopting more environmentally responsible consumption habits.
Another workshop focused on clothes sharing and sustainable fashion, encouraging young people to explore ethical
alternatives to fast fashion. A plogging activity on Margaret Island combined physical exercise with environmental
responsibility, highlighting the importance of collective action in maintaining clean public spaces. A separate workshop on
sustainable tourism allowed participants to reflect on how to minimize their environmental footprint while traveling.
Digitalization and its effects on young people are another key concern for the association. The focus is on promoting
conscious social media use, developing critical thinking skills, and encouraging screen time management. In a world where
young people are constantly connected, the association seeks to foster mindful media consumption and digital well-being.
Workshops and local events provide opportunities for young people to analyze their digital habits and develop healthier
approaches to using technology.
In addition to international projects, the association emphasizes local activities that allow members and other young people
to connect offline. Regular events such as hiking trips, soccer games, and board game nights create a welcoming
environment for social interaction, community building, and meaningful discussions.
The association is also dedicated to promoting Erasmus+, Visegrad, and other short-term mobility programs to ensure that
more young people are aware of these opportuni1es. Many young people have limited access to information about
international programs, and the association aims to make these opportunities more accessible to a broader audience. By
using social media strategically, the organization shares valuable resources, testimonials, and application guidance to
engage and inform young people in an approachable way.

Background and experience
Please briefly present the organisation / the group
Non-governmental organization Zero Waste Lutsk was founded in 2019 in the city of Lutsk, in the western part of Ukraine.
The organization started as an initiative of friends who wanted to make the world a better place. After the team refreshment
last year, the organization focuses on community development and environmental protection through educational activities
with the main goal of waste reduction. Nowadays the organization has 6 oficial members, who usually combine volunteering
in the organization together with full-time jobs. Diverse backgrounds of the members helps to strengthen weak parts of the
organization such as: recruitment and career counseling, financial management, formal education, monitoring and
evaluation etc. Aslo, as followers of the sustainable movement, by personal example we can help others to discover the
advantages of the zero waste approach in daily life. After discussing the strategy and the role of every member in Zero
Waste Lutsk NGO, the team created a common vision: organization's mission is to conduct awareness-raising, educational,
advisory, and practical activities to integrate the principles of the circular economy into the activities of residents, Lutsk City
Council, municipal institutions, and businesses. The main goal of Zero Waste Lutsk is to reduce waste through various
initiatives that include: -Educational programs. The organization conducts trainings, seminars, workshops, and lectures on
environmental awareness for different age groups, teaching people how to reduce waste in their daily lives. In total, since
2019 Zero Waste Lutsk organized more than 100 educational lectures, trainings and consultations on responsible
consumption among local self-government bodies, other NGOs and businesses, educational institutions etc. From the
recent activities in this direction we had a visit to a private school "Respublica" with an environment lesson and shared the
knowledge among the youngsters of the neighboring city Novovolynsk during the ecological festival. -Organizing events and
campaigns: Zero Waste Lutsk initiates various community events, such as clean-ups, recycling campaigns, and events that
promote environmental awareness. The history of the organization started with the Zero Waste festival, which was the only
one of this kind in the city for the last few years. - Zero Waste approach promoting. The organization works not only to
develop the infrastructure for waste sorting in the city and encourage the population to participate in waste sorting at home,
but change their minds into a zero waste approach. The media campaigns to prove it can be found on our social media
pages. -Partnerships with businesses. The organization works with local businesses to implement environmental practices
such as using reusable packaging, reducing plastic waste, and switching to eco-friendly materials. Just a month ago we
delivered a complexe 4-days training for the team of the local social enterprise misto.cafe -Implementation of sustainable
projects: Zero Waste Lutsk develops and implements projects aimed at reducing waste and conserving resources. Bright
example are 2 urban gardens in Lutsk, launched in 2023 and 2022, which help to promote composting on the local level and
solve the problem of organic food waste. These projects not only famous on the local and regional levels, but also bring
attention from the national and foreign community, that's why recently we hosted a team from Sweden to share the
knowledge about this project.

What are the activities and experience of the partner organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your
organisation’s / group’s regular youth work activities at local level.
The most activities we do are a result of the city's demand. Starting from the organization's establishment, we had a strong
partnership with city authorities and activities we organized together were always diverse, but at the same time had the
same aim — to raise awareness of the citizens about zero waste approach and creating an infrastructure for zero waste
approach implementation on the local level. Most of the time, youngsters were connected with decision making processes.
From the activities I described above, we can make a conclusion that youngsters were the beneficiaries of our activities, no
matter if it was lessons in the school or training in the restaurant. As all the Zero Waste Lutsk members belong to the age
category below 35 years old, we as an organization target similar audiences and cover the same needs as our personal and
create media campaigns based on modern trends. We also have a pool of youngsters, who connected with Zero Waste
Lutsk as volunteers. Recently one of the volunteers became a full organization board member. In July we organized our first
Erasmus+ youth exchange, which invited 5 youngsters to discuss the topic of ecology on the international level.
Please give information on the key staff/persons involved in this application and on the competences and previous
experience that they will bring to the project.
Zero Waste Lutsk team consists of 6 ecological activists, who have diverse academic, business, and non-governmental
backgrounds, but have the same goal of environment positive change. The team is involved in advocacy, monitoring and
evaluation, interaction with business and government, and the transfer of international experience to the local level. Kateryna
Demydiuk is head of Zero Waste Lutsk NGO. Since 2017 she had been living in Denmark and was so inspired by the zero
waste process and received studies, so she returned to Ukraine in 2019 to her hometown of Lutsk and created a Zero
Waste Lutsk NGO with likeminded people. She has a lot of practical experience and is familiar with European and Ukrainian
practices, therefore she has an understanding of what works better and will be able to provide relevant and accurate

Project Rationale
In this section, you are asked to explain the aims of your project, what you want to achieve and what are its expected
benefits on participants and participating organisations. Make sure to read the questions carefully and address all sub-
questions.
Needs and Objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
The motivation to carry out this project stems from the growing urgency of addressing climate change and environmental
degradation, issues that have become increasingly evident in recent years. The idea for this initiative emerged from
discussions within our NGO, where members expressed concerns about rising temperatures not only during summer but
throughout the entire year. This naturally led to conversations about air pollution, one of the primary contributors to climate
change, and the broader impact of human activities such as deforestation, urbanisation, and industrialisation. These
discussions highlighted a crucial gap in awareness—many individuals, especially young people, are not fully aware of the
extent to which their daily actions contribute to environmental degradation or what they can do to mitigate these effects.
Recognising this, we looked into existing data to better understand public perceptions of environmental challenges.
According to the Multisurvey 'Aspects of Daily Life' conducted by ISTAT between 2012 and 2021, climate change was
identified as the most pressing environmental concern by more than half (51.5%) of individuals aged 14 and over in 2021.
Air pollution followed closely behind, yet concern over these issues has slightly declined in larger municipalities in the past
three years. Waste disposal and production were also noted as significant concerns, with 44.1% of respondents expressing
worry, followed by water pollution at 40.1%, and the greenhouse effect and ozone depletion at 34.9%. Meanwhile, issues
such as noise pollution and landscape degradation ranked lower, affecting fewer than 30% of respondents. These statistics
underscore the fact that while environmental concerns are widely acknowledged, there remains a disparity between
awareness and proactive engagement in addressing these challenges. Despite the widespread recognition of climate-
related issues, our assessment revealed a lack of structured needs analysis among our target group. While members of our
organisation expressed interest in tackling environmental challenges, a formal needs assessment involving a broader
audience was not conducted prior to the development of this project. This highlights a critical shortfall—without directly
engaging young people in identifying their specific concerns, knowledge gaps, and motivations, interventions may not fully
address their needs or encourage lasting behavioral change. A structured needs assessment would provide valuable
insights into the level of environmental awareness among young people, the barriers they face in adopting sustainable
practices, and the types of activities that would most effectively engage them in climate action. To bridge this gap, the
'Natural Beauties' project aims to boost environmental awareness and action through non-formal and informal education. By
engaging young people in discussions about the impact of human activities on nature, we hope to cultivate a deeper
understanding of environmental issues and encourage proactive behaviors. The project will facilitate hands-on activities,
including practical workshops, discussions, and collaborative initiatives, enabling participants to translate their awareness
into tangible actions. Sessions such as 'Taking Care of the Environment' will focus on pollution and solutions to mitigate its
effects, while reflection activities will provide participants with an opportunity to consider their personal contributions to
environmental sustainability. One of the fundamental objectives of the project is to instill a sense of responsibility and
agency in young people. While climate change is a global issue, local actions play a crucial role in mitigating its effects.
Through participation in this project, young people will not only gain knowledge about environmental challenges but also
develop skills and strategies for integrating sustainable practices into their daily lives. Additionally, by bringing together
participants from different countries, the project creates cross-cultural exchange and cooperation, reinforcing the idea that
environmental protection is a collective responsibility that transcends national boundaries. Ultimately, this project seeks to
empower young people to become active participants in climate action. By equipping them with knowledge, tools, and a
supportive community, we aim to enable them to make informed choices and inspire change within their local contexts.
Addressing the identified gap in needs assessment, we will also incorporate participant feedback mechanisms throughout
the project lifecycle to ensure that activities remain relevant and impactful. This approach aligns with the broader goals of
the Erasmus+ programme, which emphasizes lifelong learning and youth empowerment as key drivers of social and
environmental change.
How does your project link to the objectives of the Erasmus programme and those of Youth Exchanges?
The project aligns closely with the objectives of the Erasmus+ programme and the Youth Exchange framework by nurturing
intercultural dialogue, promoting active citizenship, and empowering young people through non-formal education. Youth
exchanges provide an invaluable opportunity for young people to step beyond their local environments and engage with
peers from diverse cultural and socio-economic backgrounds, building mutual understanding and creating a sense of
European identity. Our project, Natural Beauties, leverages these opportunities by bringing together participants from
Europe, enabling them to connect, collaborate, and develop both personal and professional skills in an international setting.
One of the core goals of Erasmus+ is to enhance young people's awareness of socially and environmentally relevant issues
and inspire them to take action. Climate change is a challenge that transcends borders and requires a collective response,
making it an ideal focus for a youth exchange project. The Natural Beauties initiative directly addresses this by equipping
young participants with practical knowledge on sustainability and environmental preservation. Rather than relying solely on

Please describe the background of the participants in each participating group and how each group was formed. Please
also provide information on the group leaders, the age of the participants and how country balance is ensured. If
necessary, explain how the gender balance is respected.
The 35 participants in this project will be young people aged between 18 and 30, representing diverse backgrounds,
experiences, and motivations. Each of the five participating countries—Hungary, Poland, Portugal, Ukraine, and
Germany—will contribute a well-balanced group of participants selected based on their motivation, willingness to actively
engage, and potential to benefit from the program. The selection process will not be based solely on academic or
professional backgrounds but will focus on enthusiasm, personal development goals, and interest in environmental and
intercultural topics. To ensure a fair and structured selection, the partner organizations will lead the process in their
respective countries. Each partner has been provided with clear guidelines that emphasize inclusivity, diversity, and gender

balance. Applications will be collected through an open call, where candidates will be asked to submit a motivation letter
explaining why they want to join the project, how it aligns with their personal and professional aspirations, and how they
intend to contribute to and benefit from the exchange. Some partners may also conduct brief interviews to assess the
candidates' suitability, ensuring they are genuinely interested in the project’s objectives and willing to engage actively during
and after the program. A key priority is to provide access to young people with fewer opportunities. At least three
participants per country will come from disadvantaged backgrounds, including those facing economic, geographical, or
social barriers. Examples include young people from rural areas with limited access to educational and cultural
opportunities, individuals from low-income families who cannot afford international experiences, or those with social
difficulties, such as refugees or marginalized groups. The project will accommodate their specific needs, ensuring a
supportive and inclusive environment. Regarding group leaders, each participating country will have one experienced group
leader aged 18 or above, with no upper age limit. These individuals will be selected based on their youth work experience,
leadership skills, and ability to provide guidance and support to the participants. The group leaders will be responsible for
ensuring the well-being of their national teams, facilitating discussions, helping to resolve conflicts, and ensuring that all
participants remain engaged throughout the project. They will also serve as a bridge between the participants and the
organizing team, relaying important information and addressing any concerns. To prepare the group leaders for their roles, a
structured pre-departure training will be organized, either online or in person, before the mobility. This training will cover
their responsibilities, project objectives, conflict resolution techniques, and ways to support young people with fewer
opportunities. Additionally, a preparatory visit may be arranged for the group leaders, where they can meet the facilitators,
discuss the program in detail, and clarify expectations. Gender balance is a fundamental aspect of the project, and every
effort will be made to ensure equal representation of male and female participants. The partner organizations have
committed to selecting a balanced mix of participants, aiming for a 50-50 gender split wherever possible. However, in cases
where achieving an exact balance is not feasible due to the pool of applicants, efforts will be made to ensure a fair and
inclusive atmosphere for all participants, regardless of gender. Country balance is also a crucial consideration. Each country
will contribute six participants, including one group leader. This ensures equal representation and avoids dominance from
any particular nationality. The structure promotes an enriching intercultural exchange, allowing young people to interact with
peers from diverse backgrounds, learn from different perspectives, and develop a deeper understanding of European
identity and global challenges. Finally, two facilitators from Hungary will be responsible for guiding the learning process and
ensuring that all activities are engaging, educational, and inclusive. These facilitators have extensive experience in youth
work and working with individuals with fewer opportunities. Their role will be to moderate discussions, encourage active
participation, and adapt activities to suit the needs of all participants, making the exchange a truly transformative experience
for everyone involved.
Please describe the role and involvement of the participants from each participating group in all phases (planning before
submitting application, preparation, implementation of activities and follow-up).
Participants from each organization will play an key role in every phase of the project, ensuring their active involvement and
long-term impact. Their participation will not be limited to attending sessions but will involve meaningful contributions to
planning, execution, and follow-up actions. In the planning phase, young people from all partner organizations have already
contributed to defining the project’s theme and objectives. Through online brainstorming sessions, they shared
environmental challenges they face in their communities, helping shape relevant activities. The host organization managed
logistics, such as securing accommodation and selecting suitable venues for outdoor activities, while partner organizations
refined the methodology. Participants also provided input on cultural exchange elements, ensuring diversity in approaches.
To make the project inclusive, they suggested strategies to engage young people with fewer opportunities, including offering
translation support and adaptable learning formats. During the preparation phase, participants will take responsibility for
preparing themselves and their peers. They will conduct research on environmental sustainability and present their findings
in pre-event online meetings. Each partner organization will assign participant representatives to communicate updates and
ensure readiness. To create collaboration, participants will engage in virtual team-building activities and cultural exchange
games before meeting in person. Some will take charge of social media outreach, creating introductory posts, countdown
videos and infographics to generate excitement. Others will assist in preparing workshop materials, such as case studies on
sustainable initiatives, practical guides for eco-friendly living, and interactive activities like waste-sorting challenges.
Participants will also help plan travel arrangements, emphasizing sustainable transportation options. The host organization
will maintain structured coordination via platforms like Trello, ensuring everyone stays informed of deadlines and
responsibilities. In the implementation phase, participants will take on key roles in workshops and practical activities. They
will lead small- group discussions, facilitate sessions on environmental topics, and share their own experiences with
sustainability efforts. Hands-on activities, such as an upcycling workshop and a community clean-up day, will be participant-
driven, with each group responsible for planning and executing a segment. Some participants will document the project
through photography, videography, and daily social media updates, ensuring the wider public can engage with the project’s
progress. Daily reflection groups will encourage participants to assess their learning, share insights, and adapt activities to
better meet group needs. The cultural exchange element will include themed evenings where participants showcase
sustainable traditions from their countries, such as eco-friendly crafts, zero-waste cooking or traditional conservation
practices. Participants will also work on final project outputs, such as a sustainability action guide that will be shared digitally
for future use. In the follow-up phase, participants will continue their involvement by evaluating the project’s success and
driving local impact. They will provide feedback through structured surveys, group discussions, and individual reflections.

What will the participants learn about the chosen topic of the activity? Which learning outcomes or competences (i.e.
knowledge, skills and attitudes/behaviours) are to be acquired/improved by participants in the activity?
The Natural Beauties project provides participants with an in-depth understanding of environmental sustainability, climate
change, and the role of individuals and communities in promoting a greener future. The project aligns with the YouthPass
framework and ensures that participants engage with all eight key competences while developing practical knowledge,
skills, and attitudes that they can apply in various aspects of their lives. Through experiential learning, participants will
explore the fundamentals of sustainability, including waste reduction, upcycling, biodiversity protection, and eco-conscious
behaviors. They will gain knowledge about EU environmental policies such as the European Green Deal, the UN
Sustainable Development Goals, and local and regional environmental initiatives. Interactive sessions will allow them to
understand the connection between human activity and climate change, providing them with concrete strategies to minimize
their environmental impact. The project includes hands-on activities such as a Waste Collection and Recycling Challenge
where participants will collect waste from local public spaces and repurpose it into new functional objects, demonstrating
the principles of a circular economy. Participants will also engage in sustainable gardening workshops, where they will learn
about composting, urban gardening, and permaculture principles. A mock EU climate negotiation will enhance their
understanding of policymaking and encourage critical thinking on how different stakeholders, including governments,
NGOs, and businesses, contribute to environmental solutions. Additionally, participants will create digital campaigns, blog
posts, and video content to raise awareness about sustainability, improving their ability to communicate effectively through
different media platforms. Participants will develop essential skills, including leadership, teamwork, critical thinking, and
problem-solving. They will enhance their public speaking, debating, and advocacy skills by engaging in structured
discussions and role-playing exercises. Through project management activities, they will learn how to develop and
implement community-based environmental initiatives, strengthening their ability to plan, execute, and evaluate projects.
Digital literacy and media production skills will be supported through content creation activities where participants will
document their experiences using photography, video editing, and storytelling techniques. These skills will prepare them for
future professional and civic engagement opportunities. The project also emphasizes attitude and behavioral development.
Participants will be encouraged to adopt sustainable lifestyles by integrating small but meaningful changes into their daily
routines, such as reducing plastic consumption, using public transportation, and supporting ethical consumerism. Through
self-reflection activities, they will develop emotional intelligence, self-awareness, and resilience. The Senses and Nature
workshop will encourage mindfulness and a deeper appreciation of natural environments, promoting well-being and mental
health through nature-based activities. Participants will also engage in cross-cultural dialogue, nurturing openness,
tolerance, and an appreciation of different cultural perspectives on environmental sustainability. The project ensures that all
eight key competences are actively developed. Participants will enhance their multilingual competence through international
communication and collaboration. Personal, social, and learning-to-learn competence will be strengthened through self-
assessment and reflection sessions. Civic competence will be improved as participants learn about EU environmental
policies, activism, and sustainable community development. Entrepreneurship competence will be developed through the
Eco-Innovation Challenge, where participants will brainstorm and present innovative sustainability initiatives. Cultural
awareness and expression competence will be enhanced through storytelling, artistic workshops, and creative projects that
explore the relationship between culture and the environment. Mathematical, science, and digital competence will be
strengthened through the analysis of environmental data and the use of digital tools for advocacy and education. Literacy
competence will be improved through writing blog posts, creating informative social media content, and participating in
structured discussions. STEM and digital competence will be enhanced through the use of video editing software, mapping
tools, and data visualization techniques. At the end of the project, participants will complete their YouthPass self-
assessment, reflecting on their learning journey and linking their achievements to the key competences. The final outputs of
the project, including videos, blog posts, sustainability action plans, and policy recommendations, will serve as tangible
evidence of their learning and contributions.

The project’s activities are designed to immerse participants in experiential learning that supports deep environmental
awareness and action. The venues will include both indoor and outdoor spaces, allowing for theoretical discussions and
practical, hands-on experiences. Indoor sessions will be held in eco-friendly learning centers and community spaces, while
outdoor activities will take place in botanical gardens, forests, and urban green spaces, where participants can directly

interact with nature. The non-formal learning methods used are highly interactive, ensuring active engagement. These
include experiential learning, role-playing, storytelling, debates, hands-on workshops, gamified activities, and reflection
exercises. A strong emphasis will be placed on learning by doing, with activities designed to promote environmental
responsibility and sustainable behaviors. At the start of the project, participants will be introduced to the YouthPass
framework and the eight key competences, which will guide their learning process. Regular reflection sessions will allow
them to track their progress, set personal development goals, and integrate their learning into their daily lives. One of the key
sessions, "Discovering Nature", will engage participants in a dialogue about the meaning of nature, exploring its elements,
functions, and the human-nature connection. Facilitators will use storytelling, visuals, and guided reflection to encourage
participants to rethink their relationship with the environment. Following this, "How to Connect with Nature?" will take a more
sensory approach, incorporating blindfolded walks, nature meditation, and direct interaction with natural elements. This
session is aimed at deepening personal awareness and nurturing appreciation for the natural world. The "Gardening
Experience" will shift from observation to action. Participants will work in small groups to plant trees, cultivate herbs, and
learn composting techniques. The session will highlight urban gardening, permaculture, and sustainable food systems,
empowering participants with practical knowledge to apply in their own communities. A significant component of the project
will be the "Policy Proposal" sessions, where participants will engage in real-world problem-solving. With guidance from an
environmental policy expert, they will analyze green policies, climate action plans, and sustainability initiatives. Working in
teams, they will develop environmental policies for their local NGOs, schools, or workplaces, ensuring that their learning
translates into tangible impact beyond the project. To reinforce learning, "Eco Challenges" will be introduced, encouraging
participants to tackle environmental problems through zero-waste cooking, upcycling, and conservation initiatives. These
challenges will build teamwork, problem-solving, and leadership skills, making sustainability an engaging and accessible
topic. Throughout the project, digital engagement will play a key role. Participants will document their experiences through
photo diaries, blogs, and social media campaigns, ensuring that their learning reaches a wider audience. This will also
promote eco-conscious behaviors beyond the immediate project group. At the end of each session, structured reflection
activities will allow participants to process their learning through journals, mind maps, and peer feedback. This ensures that
they can articulate their growth and commit to concrete actions for sustainability in their communities. By combining
practical engagement, theoretical learning, personal reflection, and policy-oriented discussions, the project will equip
participants with knowledge, skills, and motivation to implement real change. The goal is not just to inform, but to empower
young people as environmental leaders, ensuring lasting impact beyond the project’s duration.

brainstorming ideas for workshops they will facilitate, and discussing sustainable initiatives they will present during the

Project Design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
Before the activity, we will ensure that participants are fully prepared through structured pre-departure arrangements, a
comprehensive Online Advanced Planning Visit (APV), and continuous support throughout and after the exchange. The
APV, held one month before the exchange, will involve team leaders, facilitators, and at least one participant per country.
This session will cover all logistical, cultural, and safety aspects, ensuring that both practical and learning objectives are
clear. Participants will receive guidance on travel, accommodation, insurance, risk management, and emergency
procedures. A detailed risk-prevention plan will be shared, including conflict resolution, cultural sensitivity, ethical behavior,
and personal safety tips. The APV will also finalize the Project Info Pack, which will include the activity schedule, travel
guidance, Youthpass information, emergency contacts, and project expectations. To promote early engagement, we will
create WhatsApp and Facebook groups where participants introduce themselves, share their motivations, and get familiar
with the project. A language support plan will be implemented to help participants with lower English proficiency. We will
provide an essential language toolkit and set up a buddy system, pairing weaker English speakers with more experienced
peers for support. Additionally, an intercultural awareness program will be conducted before departure, helping participants
understand cultural norms, differences, and potential challenges in an international group. Each participant will complete a
personal needs form, ensuring dietary preferences, medical conditions, and special learning needs are addressed. We will
also prepare a cultural adaptation guide, covering local customs, do’s and don’ts, and expected behavior. To create an
inclusive and diverse atmosphere, room allocations will ensure participants from different countries share accommodations,
nurturing deeper intercultural bonds. Support During the Activity Upon arrival, a Welcome and Orientation Session will take
place, introducing participants to the venue, project goals, house rules, and key safety contacts. To create a positive and
cooperative environment, team-building activities and ice-breaking exercises will take place immediately, allowing
participants to form connections and feel at ease. Every participant will be assigned a support mentor, either a facilitator or
team leader, to provide guidance if challenges arise. A daily well-being check-in system will be in place, where team leaders
and facilitators assess participants' emotional, social, and learning experiences, ensuring they feel safe, included, and
engaged. Non-formal learning methods such as role-playing, interactive discussions, and outdoor activities will encourage
deeper engagement. A crucial element will be Reflection Time, which will be conducted daily to allow participants to process
their experiences, set learning goals, and track personal development. These reflections will contribute to their Youthpass
certification, documenting their acquired skills. Post-Project Follow-Up and Long-Term Engagement After the activity,
participants will continue to receive structured support. A post-exchange online debriefing session will be held two weeks
later to help participants reflect on their experiences, discuss key takeaways, and explore ways to integrate their learning
into daily life. National groups will be encouraged to conduct local dissemination activities, such as presentations,
community discussions, or sustainability initiatives, to spread their newly gained knowledge. The host organization will
provide materials and support for these activities. To maintain engagement, participants will contribute to a joint digital
storytelling project, where they document their learning journey through blog posts, social media content, and video
reflections, which will be shared across Erasmus+ platforms. This will ensure broader outreach and allow future participants
to learn from their experiences. We will also establish an alumni network, inviting past participants to stay involved in
Erasmus+ initiatives and mentor new participants. Through these structured preparation, support, and follow-up strategies,
participants will not only maximize their learning and intercultural experience but also develop long-term engagement with
sustainability and youth initiatives beyond the project’s formal timeline. All participants will receive a detailed Pre-Departure
Guide covering logistics, a Code of Conduct, and a Risk Prevention Protocol with emergency contacts, venue safety rules,
and procedures for outdoor activities. An online pre-meeting will reinforce this. During the activities, 24/7 emergency access,
daily safety briefings, and first-aid packs will allow us to provide help and support when it's needed. A post-activity
debriefing and well-being check-in will ensure continued support including the evaluation process.
What measures will you put in place to ensure the safety and protection of participants?
Ensuring the safety and protection of participants is a top priority for our project. To achieve this, we have developed a
comprehensive safety plan covering all aspects of the participants’ well-being, including physical safety, health measures,
psychological support, emergency preparedness, and safeguarding against potential risks. From the moment participants
arrive, they will be provided with a detailed safety briefing that outlines key rules and procedures. This will include
emergency protocols, contact details for organizers and local authorities, and a clear explanation of behavioral expectations
to create a safe and inclusive environment. Each participant will receive an information pack with all safety-related details,
including maps, emergency contact numbers, addresses of nearby medical facilities, and guidelines on local customs and
laws. Accommodation has been carefully selected based on stringent safety requirements. The facility meets fire and health
safety standards, is equipped with emergency exits, fire extinguishers, and a first aid station, and has 24/7 security
measures in place. Additionally, a member of the organizing team will be available at all times to provide assistance and
oversee the well-being of participants. The accommodations are located within reasonable proximity to hospitals, clinics,
and pharmacies to ensure that medical assistance is readily available if needed. During travel and transportation, safety is
ensured by organizing group journeys rather than allowing individuals to travel alone. Public transportation will be used
where possible, with organizers ensuring that all participants are familiar with routes, schedules, and safe traveling
practices. A designated responsible person from the team will accompany participants on all organized trips to ensure no

one gets lost and to provide immediate support if needed. For additional security, a local native speaker will be present to
help with communication barriers and provide assistance in case of misunderstandings or emergencies. Before arrival,
participants will be required to complete a health declaration form, disclosing any pre-existing conditions, allergies, or
required medications. Organizers will then ensure that necessary medical facilities and pharmacies are accessible. First aid
kits will be available at all activity locations, and at least one trained first aider will be present at all times. Moreover,
participants will be advised to obtain adequate health insurance coverage, including travel and accident insurance. For EU
citizens, having a European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) is strongly encouraged to facilitate access to medical services.
Emergency preparedness is a key component of our safety strategy. Each participant will be briefed on emergency
response procedures, including evacuation plans, fire safety measures, and what to do in case of an accident. Emergency
contact numbers for medical services, police, and fire departments will be clearly displayed in common areas and activity
spaces. A dedicated vehicle will be on standby for urgent medical transport if required. For the duration of the project, all
activities have been carefully designed to minimize risks. Outdoor and physical activities, such as gardening, hiking, or
waste collection, will be supervised by trained professionals. Participants will be provided with necessary protective
equipment, including gloves, appropriate footwear, and sun protection. In cases of extreme weather conditions, alternative
indoor activities will be arranged to avoid any risk of exposure to harsh elements. Mental well-being is just as important as
physical safety. To support participants' psychological health, the program includes mindfulness and relaxation sessions,
opportunities for self-reflection, and designated quiet spaces where individuals can unwind. If a participant experiences
emotional distress, our team members, trained in basic mental health first aid, will be available for support and guidance.
Additionally, participants will have the opportunity to provide anonymous feedback regarding their well-being throughout the
program, allowing organizers to address any concerns promptly. A strict safeguarding policy will be enforced to ensure that
all interactions are respectful and that every participant feels secure. Clear guidelines will be in place to prevent harassment,
bullying, or any form of discrimination. A confidential reporting mechanism will be established so that participants can raise
concerns without fear of retaliation. The organizing team will also conduct regular check-ins with participants to ensure their
comfort and safety. Finally, communication will be maintained at all times through a group messaging system where
updates, reminders, and emergency alerts can be shared instantly. This will allow organizers to quickly address any arising
issues and keep participants informed about any necessary safety measures.
What activities are foreseen after the end of the Youth Exchange? How will the participants follow-up on the activity?
After the end of the Youth Exchange, several structured follow-up activities will ensure that the impact of the project extends
beyond the participants and the immediate project duration. Each participating organisation will be responsible for guiding
their respective groups through a well-defined dissemination and engagement plan that reinforces the lessons learned and
maintains the momentum of the exchange. Participants will take an active role in sharing their experiences within their local
communities, educational institutions, and youth organisations. To achieve this, they will organise thematic workshops,
storytelling sessions, and panel discussions at schools, universities, and community centres. These events will not only
highlight their newly acquired knowledge on sustainability and Erasmus+ opportunities but also encourage other young
people to engage in similar initiatives. The participating organisations will provide logistical support, materials, and guidance
to ensure the effectiveness of these activities. One of the key follow-up activities will be a peer-to-peer mentoring initiative,
where participants will mentor younger peers or new volunteers in their organisations. This will help them apply their
knowledge in a real-world setting and enhance their leadership, communication, and organisational skills. Additionally, they
will be encouraged to use creative tools such as podcasts, blogs, or short documentary-style videos to share their insights in
an engaging and accessible format. Each participant group will be tasked with designing a small-scale environmental
initiative in their home community. This could involve activities like local clean-up campaigns, sustainability awareness
sessions, or the creation of urban green spaces. These initiatives will serve as a practical way to reinforce their commitment
to sustainability while creating community engagement. The partner organisations will provide support by helping
participants connect with local stakeholders, securing necessary permissions, and sourcing materials or funding where
needed. To ensure that the impact of the project is measurable, participants will complete individual reflection reports and
feedback surveys detailing how they have applied their learning since the exchange. They will also be invited to contribute to
a collective impact report, which will be compiled and published online. This report will highlight key learnings, best
practices, and recommendations for future youth projects. A follow-up online networking meeting will be held three months
after the project ends, bringing together all participants and partner organisations. This will serve as a platform for sharing
progress updates, discussing challenges faced during local activities, and identifying opportunities for future collaboration.
This meeting will also be used to plan the submission of future Erasmus+ project proposals based on the participants’ ideas
and interests developed during the exchange. Social media engagement will remain a crucial part of the follow-up strategy.
Participants will be encouraged to regularly post about their follow-up activities using a common hashtag, allowing for real-
time tracking of their impact. Dedicated content such as vlogs, interviews, and “day-in-the-life” features of participants
implementing sustainability in their daily lives will be shared across platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and Facebook to
maintain visibility. Partner organisations will also support this by running monthly thematic campaigns, featuring participants’
achievements, and engaging local influencers to amplify the message. Another major follow-up activity will be the
development of an online resource hub where all project materials, participant testimonies, and practical guides on
sustainability and non-formal education methods will be made freely available. This will allow other youth workers,
educators, and organisations to build upon the project’s outcomes and integrate them into their own activities. Finally, an
end-of-year hybrid event will be organised by the coordinating organisation, where participants and representatives from
partner organisations will present the long-term impact of the exchange. This event will include a showcase of participants’
initiatives, expert panels on youth sustainability projects, and networking opportunities for potential future collaborations.

Recognition of learning outcomes
How will you support participants to be aware of what they have learned and which competences they have developed or
improved? Please remember to include the methods that support reflection and documentation of the learning outcomes in
the daily timetable of each activity.
Throughout the project, we will ensure that participants are fully aware of their learning progress, skill development, and
competence improvement by integrating structured reflection, interactive learning documentation, and YouthPass validation
into the daily schedule. The recognition of learning outcomes will not only be a closing step at the end of the exchange but
will be a continuous, dynamic process that supports self-awareness, personal growth, and peer support. To begin with,
participants will be introduced to the concept of YouthPass on the very first day. A dedicated session will clarify its purpose
as a tool for recognizing and validating non-formal and informal learning within Erasmus+ projects. Instead of passively
receiving information, participants will actively engage in identifying the eight key competences that YouthPass focuses on:
Multilingual competence, Personal, social, and learning-to-learn competence, Citizenship competence, Entrepreneurship
competence, Cultural awareness and expression competence, Mathematical competence and competence in science,
technology, and engineering (STEM), Literacy competence, and Digital competence. They will work in small groups using
interactive methods such as role-playing, debates, and brainstorming sessions to connect these competences to their
personal goals and project activities. From day one, participants will be encouraged to set personal learning goals related to
these competences. To make this process more engaging and accessible, we will introduce individual learning diaries or
reflection journals where participants will write their expectations, track their progress, and note key insights. These journals
will serve as a private space for self- reflection, helping them structure their thoughts and recognize their improvements.
Every evening, we will hold a structured "Reflection Circle," which will be an integral part of the daily routine. This session
will not only allow participants to summarize what they have learned during the day but will also encourage them to link their
learning outcomes to the key competences outlined in YouthPass. The Reflection Circle will be guided by facilitators using
targeted questions that challenge participants to think critically about their experiences. Some examples of these reflective
prompts include: "What was the most surprising thing you learned today?" "How did today's activities challenge your existing
knowledge or skills?" "Which of the eight key competences do you think you have developed today, and how?" "How can
you apply what you learned today in your daily life or future projects?" To make the reflection process even more interactive
and supportive, we will also use peer-to-peer feedback. Participants will be encouraged to give constructive feedback to
each other about their strengths, contributions, and areas for improvement. This method not only boosts motivation but also
helps develop social and communication skills. A particularly powerful tool we will implement is "Competence Mapping."
This will take place in the middle and at the end of the project, where participants will create their own competence maps,
visually representing how they have developed throughout the experience. They will use a spider diagram, timeline, or mind
map to track their progress, identifying areas where they have improved and areas they still wish to work on. These
competence maps will be reviewed in pairs or small groups, allowing for further discussion and peer validation of their
learning. Another effective method we will incorporate is "Skill Storytelling." In this exercise, participants will create short
narratives about a situation where they learned something new or overcame a challenge during the exchange. These stories
will be shared in small groups and later documented as part of their YouthPass reflection. By narrating their experiences,
participants will solidify their understanding of how learning took place and how it can be transferred to real-life situations.
Midway through the project, we will also have a "Checkpoint Reflection," where facilitators and group leaders will conduct
one-on-one or small group discussions with participants to help them assess their learning journey so far. This will serve as
an opportunity to adjust learning goals if necessary and to provide additional support to those who may need guidance in
recognizing their progress. On the final day of the exchange, we will hold a structured YouthPass workshop. Participants will
work individually and in small groups to complete their YouthPass certificates, identifying the skills they have gained and
how they have developed each competence. This will not be just a formal step but a highly engaging and empowering
process where they receive feedback from facilitators and peers to refine their learning statements. To celebrate and
reinforce the importance of their learning journey, we will conclude with a YouthPass ceremony.

If any, please explain the particular measures (accompanying person, reinforced preparation etc.) you will put in place to
cater for the specific needs of these participants and/or to support their participation.
To effectively support the participation of young people facing various challenges, we have developed a series of specific
measures aimed at addressing barriers related to education, culture, economics, geography and social factors. Each
participant's journey in this project is crucial, and we aim to ensure that everyone feels included, empowered and capable of
benefiting from the experience. One of the key measures we will implement is assigning an accompanying person to each
participant, especially those with fewer opportunities. This person will serve as a mentor and support figure throughout the
project. They will assist in navigating any challenges that arise, whether they relate to emotional concerns, cultural
differences or logistical issues. The team leaders will be in role of the accompanying person, they will be responsible for
checking in with participants regularly to provide encouragement and guidance, ensuring they feel comfortable and included
in all activities. We understand that preparation is vital for participants who might feel anxious about joining a new program.
Therefore, we will offer reinforced preparation sessions before the project starts. These sessions will include personal
meetings, either online or in-person, with team leaders who will explain the Erasmus+ program, its goals and the
expectations for participants. By having clear communication about what to expect, participants can feel more at ease and
ready to engage. Additionally, we will provide comprehensive information through an infopack, which will outline the
project's activities, objectives and support resources. Recognizing that cultural differences can pose challenges, we will
conduct cultural sensitivity training for all participants. This training will help them understand and appreciate each other's
backgrounds and traditions, building mutual respect and cooperation. Activities like "Cultural Nights" will encourage
participants to share their cultures, promoting integration and understanding. This exchange of knowledge will enable
everyone to feel valued and included in the group. Economic barriers can prevent many young people from participating
fully. To combat this, we will guarantee financial support throughout the project. This includes covering transportation costs
to and from activities. If participants face difficulties purchasing tickets, we will reimburse them after the project to alleviate
financial strain. Importantly, there will be no participation fees for those with fewer opportunities, ensuring that money does
not hinder anyone’s involvement. Participants who live far from the project location may struggle with transportation and
feelings of isolation. To mitigate these geographical barriers, we will facilitate connections between national and
international teams. Team leaders will help participants build networks before the project begins, enabling them to
communicate and bond with others. We will also encourage group travel to reduce carbon footprints and support teamwork.
Traveling together can create a sense of camaraderie, making participants feel more comfortable in new settings. Many
participants may be excluded from traditional higher education or training systems. To address this, our activities will focus
on non-formal education methods. This approach allows young people to learn in engaging, hands-on ways. For example,
the "Observation of Nature" activity encourages exploration and learning about the environment directly, helping participants
develop a deeper understanding of their surroundings. This experiential learning is particularly beneficial for mental health,
as it promotes awareness and connection with nature. Mental health is as important as financial support. We will ensure that
participants have access to mental health resources, including reflection time and the availability of their team leaders for
support. Regular check-ins will provide a space for participants to express their thoughts and feelings, helping them
navigate any challenges they may encounter. Team leaders will be trained to recognize signs of stress or anxiety and will be
equipped to offer assistance when needed. We are committed to implementing specific measures to cater to the unique
needs of participants with fewer opportunities. By reinforced preparation, cultural sensitivity training, financial support,
geographical connections, educational opportunities and mental health resources, we will create an inclusive and supportive
environment. Our goal is to empower every young person involved in this project, allowing them to thrive and grow while
minimizing barriers. Through these thoughtful approaches, we aim to break down the social, cultural, economic and
educational barriers that may prevent young people from fully participating in this enriching experience.

Virtual learning/Blended activities and use of virtual components

The virtual learning and blended activities will be an integral part of our project, ensuring that 100% of participants engage
in digital components before, during, and after the youth exchange. Virtual components will not only facilitate communication
but also enhance collaboration, accessibility, and engagement, making learning more interactive while promoting
sustainability. One of the most important virtual elements is the online Advance Planning Visit (APV), which will be held
approximately two months before the youth exchange. This meeting will bring together group leaders and facilitators from

Meet and will cover key topics such as project objectives, timeline, division of responsibilities, risk assessment, participant
selection criteria, and budget management. It will also include a dedicated session on ensuring equal participation,
accessibility, and conflict resolution strategies. The online APV allows all partners to establish a shared understanding of the
project, helping to prevent miscommunication and logistical issues later on. Before the exchange, participants will engage in
various online activities designed to build group cohesion and prepare them for the experience. A private Facebook group
and a WhatsApp community will serve as the main communication channels, where participants can introduce themselves,
share personal interests, and engage in discussions about the project’s themes. Interactive platforms like Miro or Padlet will
be used to create a collaborative online space where participants can post their expectations, fears, and contributions.
Additionally, participants will take part in virtual icebreaker activities such as short introduction videos, cultural sharing

Environmental friendly practices
Will you include sustainable and environmental-friendly practices in your activities?
Yes
Please describe them and mention how will you raise the awareness of participants on these sustainable practices?
Environmental sustainability will be at the core of this project, ensuring participants gain both theoretical knowledge and
practical skills to implement eco-friendly habits in their daily lives. We will integrate sustainability into every aspect of the
program, from transportation and food consumption to waste management and resource efficiency, creating a holistic
approach to green living. To reduce the carbon footprint of travel, participants will be encouraged to use trains and buses
where feasible, while those who must fly will be informed about carbon offsetting programs. Upon arrival, all in-country travel
will prioritize walking, biking, or public transportation. Short-distance travel between venues will be incorporated into the
program through guided "Walk & Talk" discussions on sustainability. Carpooling will be arranged when necessary, ensuring
minimal vehicle use. Food sustainability will be actively promoted through conscious consumption and waste reduction.
Instead of merely controlling portion sizes, we will implement a "Zero-Waste Kitchen Challenge," requiring participants to
plan and cook meals using locally sourced, seasonal ingredients while minimizing waste. Meals will emphasize plant-based
foods to reduce the environmental impact of meat consumption. Any leftover food will be shared or repurposed into future
meals, and a food- sharing station will allow participants to redistribute any surplus. The "Local Dinners" activity will highlight
farm-to-table principles by using homegrown produce from the gardening workshops, where participants will plant and
nurture herbs and vegetables for consumption. Waste management will follow a strict "Zero Single-Use Plastic" policy. No
disposable bottles, cutlery, or cups will be provided. Instead, each participant will receive a reusable water bottle, cloth
napkin, and tote bag. Waste segregation will be enforced, with color-coded bins available at all venues. A friendly "Eco
Challenge" will reward participants who reduce waste, compost food scraps, and upcycle materials. Creative workshops will
repurpose discarded items into useful products, reinforcing sustainability through hands-on engagement. Resource
efficiency will also be prioritized. A paperless policy will be implemented using Google Drive for tickets, schedules, and
learning materials, eliminating unnecessary printing. Energy and water conservation practices will be promoted, with
reminders to turn off lights, take shorter showers, and reuse towels and linens. During accommodation, participants will be
encouraged to track their daily energy and water use, nurturing long-term awareness. Sustainability awareness will be
reinforced through structured discussions, interactive activities, and personal commitments. Daily reflection sessions will
include sustainability check-ins, encouraging participants to evaluate their eco-friendly choices. Engaging debates and role-
playing activities will challenge them to think critically about global environmental issues and develop practical solutions. At
the end of the program, participants will create a "Sustainability Commitment Plan," outlining eco-friendly habits they pledge
to continue post-exchange. These commitments will be compiled into a digital resource, shared through partner
organizations and social media to inspire broader change. By integrating sustainability into every facet of the program,
participants will leave with the skills and motivation to adopt greener lifestyles. The project’s impact will extend beyond its
duration, as participants bring sustainable practices to their communities, nurturing long-term environmental consciousness.
